title: 2007 The Mystery of the Bayon Face Towers
highlights:
essay examines the cultural and religious shifts in the Bàyon temple complex during and after the reign of Jayavarma Parameśvara, focusing on the iconography of the face towers. It questions the interpretations of the faces as Buddhist or Shaiva and discusses evidence of religious tolerance and the alterations made post-Jayavarman VII. title: Angkor Thom, The Great City - Smarthistory
highlights: East face of the Baphuon. Angkor Thom, Siem Reap, Cambodia (photo: LBM1948, CC BY-SA 4.0) East face of the Baphuon, Angkor Thom, Siem Reap, Cambodia (photo: LBM1948, CC BY-SA 4.0)  With each successive king came the building of a new royal city such as Angkor Thom. The name, Angkor Thom, translated from theKhmerlanguagemeans “Great Kingdom,” alluding to the grand ambitions of its patron:King Jayavarman VII. Upon coming to the throne, Jayavarman VII quickly began the process of building a new royal city (Angkor Thom) and state temple (Bayon) in the late 12th century because he understood, like Khmer rulers before him, that the capital would serve as the political  ...    Bayon, the most notable temple at Angkor Thom (photo: Dmitry A. title: Bayon
highlights:
Angkor in Cambodia. Built in the late 12th or early 13th century as the state temple of King Jayavarman VII(Khmer: ព្រះបាទជ័យវរ្ម័នទី ៧), it stands at the centre of Jayavarman’s capital city, Angkor Thom(Khmer: អង្គរធំ). The Bayon reflects the strong Buddhist orientation of Jayavarman VII’s reign. Originally conceived as a Mahayana Buddhist monument centered on the cult of the  ...    The temple is best known for its many towers adorned with serene, smiling stone faces carved on all sides, which are commonly interpreted as representations of King Jayavarman VII and symbolize compassion and royal authority.
